places.name: The Witchery by the Castle
places.reviewSummary: A theatrically gothic restaurant draped in candlelight and tapestries, steps from the castle esplanade. Famous for indulgent Scottish dishes and an encyclopedic wine list.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the dark romantic atmosphere and service, though some note the prices are steep for the portions.
places.localTip: Ask for a table in the Secret Garden room downstairs — it is brighter and even more atmospheric than the main Witchery dining room.
places.story: Opened in 1979 in a 16th-century merchant's house, the Witchery takes its name from the hundreds of women burned as witches on Castlehill nearby. Step inside and you are wrapped in oak panelling, gilded leather and flickering candles that have charmed everyone from Hollywood stars to honeymooners. It was once voted one of the world's most romantic restaurants, and a single evening here feels like dining inside a spell.
places.highlight: Don't miss the over-the-top theatrical Secret Garden room.

places.name: The Kitchin
places.reviewSummary: Tom Kitchin's flagship, a Michelin-starred temple to 'from nature to plate' Scottish produce in a converted Leith whisky bond. Seasonal menus change constantly with the catch and harvest.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests consistently praise the impeccable seasonal cooking and warm service, with a few finding it a serious financial commitment.
places.localTip: Book weeks ahead and request the lunch menu — it is far better value than dinner for the same kitchen.
places.story: Tom Kitchin was the youngest Scottish chef to win a Michelin star when he opened here in 2006, aged just 29. Set in an old Leith whisky warehouse on the waterfront, the open kitchen lets you watch the brigade work while the menu literally changes with the tides and seasons. His 'from nature to plate' philosophy turned this dockside corner into one of Scotland's most celebrated tables.

places.name: Ondine Oyster & Grill
places.reviewSummary: A buzzy first-floor seafood restaurant on George IV Bridge with a curving crustacean bar and sustainably sourced shellfish. Roy Brett's kitchen is a benchmark for Scottish seafood.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners love the ultra-fresh oysters and lobster, though tables near the bar can get loud at peak times.
places.localTip: Sit at the horseshoe Crustacean Bar and order the seafood platter with a glass of Picpoul.
places.story: Chef Roy Brett trained with Rick Stein before opening Ondine in 2009 above the Royal Mile. Everything here champions Scotland's astonishing coastline, from Loch Fyne oysters to langoustines hauled the same morning. It has become the place locals send visitors who want to understand just how good Scottish seafood can be.

places.name: The Albanach
places.address: 197 High Street, Royal Mile, Edinburgh EH1 1PE
places.reviewSummary: A Royal Mile bar and restaurant serving textbook haggis, neeps and tatties alongside a serious whisky wall. A reliable spot for first-timers wanting the classics done well.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the friendly staff and authentic haggis, while a few mention it gets packed during festival season.
places.localTip: Order the haggis with a wee dram of Highland whisky poured over it — staff will recommend a pairing.
places.story: Albanach means 'Scotsman' in Gaelic, and this Royal Mile institution leans proudly into that name. The walls are lined with hundreds of whiskies and the kitchen turns out the dishes Robert Burns would have recognised. It is the kind of cosy stone-walled room where a rainy Edinburgh afternoon slips happily away.
places.highlight: Don't miss a whisky flight from the wall of over 100 bottles.

places.name: Dishoom Edinburgh
places.reviewSummary: The Edinburgh outpost of the beloved Bombay-café chain, set in a grand former bank on St Andrew Square. Famed for black daal, bacon naan rolls and a glamorous interior.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests adore the black daal and atmosphere, though the no-reservations dinner queue can be long.
places.localTip: Go for breakfast and order the bacon naan roll — there is no queue in the morning and it is one of the best things in the city.
places.story: Dishoom recreates the old Irani cafés of Bombay, those faded all-day institutions where everyone from poets to taxi drivers once gathered. The Edinburgh branch sits in a palatial former bank, all marble and ceiling fans, and its 24-hour-cooked black daal has a cult following. Each branch quietly raises money to feed hungry children for every meal served.

places.name: The Fishmarket Newhaven
places.reviewSummary: A harbourside fish-and-chip restaurant and takeaway on Newhaven's historic pier, run by a fourth-generation fish merchant. Some of the freshest battered haddock in the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the crisp fish and harbour setting, though the sit-down restaurant can have a wait at weekends.
places.localTip: Get the takeaway counter, not the restaurant, and eat your fish supper on the pier looking across to Fife.
places.story: Newhaven was once Edinburgh's bustling fishing harbour, where 'fishwives' in striped skirts famously hawked the catch through the city streets. The Fishmarket is run by the Welch family, fishmongers here for generations, so the haddock barely travels before it hits the fryer. Eating chips on the old stone pier with the lighthouse in view is about as Edinburgh as it gets.
places.highlight: Don't miss the smoked haddock or the daily-changing fresh fish special.

places.name: Aizle
places.reviewSummary: An inventive restaurant inside the Kimpton Charlotte Square hotel where the menu is a no-choice 'harvest' of seasonal Scottish ingredients revealed as you dine. Hyper-seasonal and deeply local.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise the creativity and value of the tasting menu, with a few wishing for more menu choice.
places.localTip: Mention dietary needs when booking — they tailor the surprise menu beautifully but need notice.
places.story: Aizle is an old Scots word for a glowing ember, and the kitchen burns with that same quiet intensity. Rather than a menu, you are handed a list of the month's harvest and simply trust the chefs. The result is some of the most thoughtful seasonal cooking in Scotland, served in a serene Georgian townhouse just off Charlotte Square.

places.name: Wedgwood the Restaurant
places.reviewSummary: An intimate fine-dining restaurant on the lower Royal Mile where chef Paul Wedgwood forages many of his own ingredients. Polished Scottish cooking without the stiffness.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests consistently highlight the foraged touches and unpretentious service, though the dining room is small so booking is essential.
places.localTip: Try the foraged tasting menu and ask Paul about the ingredients he picked himself that week.
places.story: Paul Wedgwood is a keen forager who heads into the Pentland Hills and Scottish coast for the wild garlic, sea herbs and mushrooms that lift his plates. His snug restaurant near Holyrood has built a devoted following for cooking that is ambitious yet warmly welcoming. It is proof that some of Edinburgh's best food hides quietly at the bottom of the Royal Mile.

places.name: The Elephant House
places.reviewSummary: The 'birthplace of Harry Potter' café where J.K. Rowling wrote early chapters gazing at the castle. A pilgrimage spot for Potter fans, with views over Greyfriars.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the literary history and castle views, though many note it is touristy and was rebuilt after a 2021 fire.
places.localTip: Sit in the back room for the castle-and-Greyfriars view Rowling herself looked out on while writing.
places.story: In the 1990s a struggling single mother nursed coffees in the back room here, scribbling a story about a boy wizard while staring up at Edinburgh Castle. That writer was J.K. Rowling, and the Elephant House proudly calls itself the birthplace of Harry Potter. After a fire in 2021 the café was lovingly restored, and the graffiti-covered toilet walls of fan messages have become legend.
places.highlight: Don't miss the castle view from the back window.

places.name: Lowdown Coffee
places.reviewSummary: A sleek basement coffee bar beneath George Street, beloved by purists for meticulous brews and minimalist design. One of the city's best for single-origin filter.
places.googleReviewSummary: Coffee lovers praise the precision and calm space, though some find the minimalist menu limited if you want food.
places.localTip: Order the filter flight to taste the day's beans side by side — the baristas love talking through them.
places.story: Tucked down a quiet stair off George Street, Lowdown is a hushed, design-led retreat from the New Town bustle. The owners obsess over sourcing and brewing, treating each cup like a small ceremony. It is the café Edinburgh's coffee geeks send each other to when they want the real thing.

places.name: Söderberg Pavilion
places.reviewSummary: A bright Swedish bakery-café in a glass pavilion at Quartermile, famed for cardamom buns and sourdough. A favourite morning stop near the Meadows.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the cinnamon and cardamom buns, though seating fills quickly on weekend mornings.
places.localTip: Grab a cardamom bun and a coffee to take across to the Meadows on a sunny day.
places.story: Söderberg brought proper Swedish fika culture to Edinburgh, that beloved ritual of coffee paired with a sweet bun and a pause in the day. The glass pavilion at Quartermile sits where the old Royal Infirmary once stood, now a buzzing student and family district. Their cardamom buns have a near-fanatical local following.

places.name: Hula Juice Bar & Gallery
places.reviewSummary: A cheerful juice bar and brunch spot on curving Victoria Street, serving smoothie bowls, avocado toast and cold-pressed juices. A bright, healthy break from heavier fare.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the fresh juices and friendly vibe, though the space is small and can get cramped.
places.localTip: Get an açai bowl and snag the window bench for people-watching down Victoria Street.
places.story: Sitting on Victoria Street, the famously curved, candy-coloured row said to have inspired Harry Potter's Diagon Alley, Hula is a burst of fresh energy. It pairs cold-pressed juices and brunch bowls with a rotating wall of local art. After a heavy Scottish breakfast elsewhere, it is where locals come to feel virtuous again.

places.name: Clarinda's Tea Room
places.reviewSummary: A delightfully old-fashioned tearoom near Holyrood with floral china, homemade scones and proper pots of tea. A nostalgic refuge on the lower Royal Mile.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests adore the vintage charm and home baking, though the tiny room means you may wait for a table.
places.localTip: Order a cream tea with a fresh-baked scone — and have cash handy, as it is delightfully old-school.
places.story: Clarinda's has been pouring tea near the bottom of the Royal Mile for decades, a cosy world of chintz, teapots and trays of warm scones. Its name nods to 'Clarinda', the pen-name of Agnes Maclehose, the Edinburgh woman Robert Burns famously adored. Stepping inside feels like time-travelling to a gentler, scone-scented Scotland.

places.name: Cairngorm Coffee (Frederick Street)
places.reviewSummary: A snug basement café from a much-loved local roaster, known for excellent flat whites and legendary grilled-cheese toasties. A cult New Town favourite.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the coffee and cheese toasties, though the basement space is small and seating limited.
places.localTip: Order the cheese toastie with your coffee — it is the not-so-secret reason regulars keep coming back.
places.story: Cairngorm started as a tiny basement obsession with great coffee and has grown into one of Edinburgh's favourite local roasters. Named for the rugged Cairngorm mountains, it keeps things unfussy: superb beans and a grilled cheese that locals will queue for. It is the kind of place that quietly defines a neighbourhood's mornings.

places.name: Edinburgh Castle
places.reviewSummary: The fortress crowning the city's volcanic rock, home to the Scottish Crown Jewels, the Stone of Destiny and the One O'Clock Gun. The defining symbol of Edinburgh.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call it a must-see with stunning views, though many warn it gets very crowded and tickets should be booked online in advance.
places.localTip: Book the first entry slot of the day online to walk the esplanade before the crowds arrive.
places.story: Perched on an extinct volcano, Edinburgh Castle has been a royal stronghold for over 900 years and is one of the most besieged places in the world. Inside lie Scotland's Crown Jewels and the Stone of Destiny on which kings were crowned, while St Margaret's Chapel is the city's oldest building. Every day but Sunday the One O'Clock Gun still booms across the city, a tradition begun so ships in the Forth could set their clocks.
places.highlight: Don't miss the One O'Clock Gun firing at 1pm sharp.

places.name: Palace of Holyroodhouse
places.reviewSummary: The King's official Scottish residence at the foot of the Royal Mile, steeped in the drama of Mary, Queen of Scots. State apootments, ruined abbey and gardens beneath Arthur's Seat.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the history and audio guide, though it can close at short notice for royal events.
places.localTip: Check the website before visiting, as the palace closes for official royal duties several times a year.
places.story: Holyroodhouse began as a 12th-century abbey and grew into the Stuart kings' palace, the stage for Scotland's bloodiest royal drama. It was here in 1566 that Mary, Queen of Scots watched her secretary David Rizzio stabbed to death before her, and a plaque still marks the spot. Today it remains the monarch's official residence in Scotland, with the romantic ruined abbey open to the skies behind it.
places.highlight: Don't miss Mary, Queen of Scots' chambers and the roofless abbey.

places.name: The Royal Mile
places.address: High Street, Old Town, Edinburgh EH1 1RE
places.reviewSummary: The historic spine of the Old Town running from the castle down to Holyrood, lined with closes, kilt shops, pubs and street performers. The beating heart of medieval Edinburgh.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the atmosphere and hidden closes, though many note it is heavily touristy with persistent kilt-shop touts.
places.localTip: Duck into the narrow closes off the main street — they hide quiet courtyards and far fewer tourists.
places.story: The Royal Mile is exactly that: a mile of ancient street linking castle to palace, the processional route of Scottish monarchs. For centuries the city's people were crammed into towering tenements along here, some rising ten storeys, in what was one of the most densely packed cities in Europe. Today its closes and wynds still whisper tales of plague, poets and Jekyll-and-Hyde double lives.
places.highlight: Don't miss exploring the dark, narrow closes branching off either side.

places.name: Arthur's Seat
places.reviewSummary: An extinct volcano rising 250m in the heart of the city, offering Edinburgh's finest panoramic views after a short but steep climb. A wild Highland summit minutes from the Old Town.
places.googleReviewSummary: Hikers call the summit views unbeatable, though they warn the paths get muddy, windy and slippery in wet weather.
places.localTip: Climb for sunrise or sunset and take the gentler path from Dunsapie Loch if you want an easier ascent.
places.story: Arthur's Seat is the remains of a volcano that erupted 350 million years ago, later carved by glaciers into the rugged peak you see today. Legends tie it to King Arthur and to Camelot, and its slopes hide an ancient hillfort and a mysterious set of miniature coffins found in 1836. From the top, the whole city, the Firth of Forth and the hills of Fife spread out beneath your feet.
places.highlight: Don't miss the summit view stretching to the Forth bridges.

places.name: National Museum of Scotland
places.reviewSummary: A vast, free museum spanning Scottish history, world cultures, science and nature under a soaring Victorian glass roof. Home to Dolly the Sheep and a stunning rooftop terrace.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the breadth and the free entry, though many say you need most of a day and it gets busy on rainy days.
places.localTip: Take the lift to the seventh-floor roof terrace for one of the best free castle views in the city.
places.story: This museum merges a soaring Victorian iron-and-glass hall with a striking modern wing, telling the story of Scotland and the wider world for free. Among its treasures is Dolly the Sheep, the first mammal cloned from an adult cell, born just outside Edinburgh in 1996. Children sprint between fossils, a flying machine and ancient Egyptian relics, all beneath that breathtaking glass roof.
places.highlight: Don't miss Dolly the Sheep and the rooftop terrace.

places.name: Royal Botanic Garden Edinburgh
places.reviewSummary: A 70-acre botanic garden dating to 1670, with Victorian glasshouses, a rock garden and a famous city-skyline view across the lawns. A serene green escape north of the centre.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the peaceful grounds and skyline view, though some note the historic Glasshouses have had restoration closures.
places.localTip: Walk to the viewpoint on the lawn near Inverleith House for a postcard view of the castle and Old Town.
places.story: Founded in 1670 as a physic garden to grow medicinal plants, the Botanics is one of the oldest scientific gardens in Britain. It later moved to Inverleith, where 70 lush acres now hold plants from across the globe and towering Victorian glasshouses. Generations of Edinburgh families have learned to walk, picnic and fall in love among its rhododendrons.

places.name: Scott Monument
places.address: E Princes Street Gardens, Edinburgh EH2 2EJ
places.reviewSummary: A soaring Gothic spire on Princes Street honouring author Sir Walter Scott, with 287 twisting steps to viewing platforms over the city. The largest monument to a writer in the world.
places.googleReviewSummary: Climbers love the views and quirky narrow staircase, though those with claustrophobia find the upper stairs very tight.
places.localTip: The final spiral staircase is extremely narrow — go early to avoid awkward two-way traffic on the steps.
places.story: Completed in 1844, this blackened Gothic rocket is the largest monument to a writer anywhere on earth, built for Sir Walter Scott, author of Ivanhoe and Waverley. Climb its 287 increasingly narrow steps and the city unfurls beneath you, level by level. Its sooty colour comes from the local sandstone reacting over centuries, giving it its brooding, almost gothic-cathedral look.
places.highlight: Don't miss the topmost viewing gallery if you can squeeze up the final stair.

places.name: Royal Yacht Britannia
places.address: Ocean Terminal, Ocean Drive, Leith, Edinburgh EH6 6JJ
places.reviewSummary: The former royal yacht, now a beautifully preserved floating museum in Leith. Tour the Queen's private quarters, the bridge and the engine room with an excellent audio guide.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors consistently rate it among Edinburgh's best attractions for its intimate royal insight, with only the dockside-mall location drawing mild grumbles.
places.localTip: Stop for tea and cake in the Royal Deck Tea Room — it has lovely views over the water.
places.story: For 44 years Britannia carried the Royal Family over a million nautical miles, from honeymoons to state visits, before being retired in 1997. The Queen reportedly considered it the one place she could truly relax, and the surprisingly homely interiors reflect that. Wandering the decks and peeking into the modest royal bedrooms feels like a strangely intimate royal encounter.

places.name: The Meadows
places.reviewSummary: A broad, tree-lined parkland beloved by students and families, with cherry-blossom avenues, BBQs in summer and a relaxed local buzz. Edinburgh's communal back garden.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the open green space and blossom season, though it gets very busy and litter-strewn on warm summer evenings.
places.localTip: Come in late April for the cherry-blossom tunnel along Middle Meadow Walk — it is breathtaking.
places.story: The Meadows was once a shallow loch, drained in the 18th century to create the open green you see today. Now it is where the whole city exhales: students sprawl with books, families fly kites, and in spring the blossom-lined walks turn pink. It sits right by the university, giving the area a youthful, easygoing heartbeat.
places.highlight: Don't miss the cherry blossom in late April.

places.name: Stockbridge
places.address: St Stephen Street, Stockbridge, Edinburgh EH3 5AB
places.reviewSummary: A village-like neighbourhood of independent shops, delis and bars beside the Water of Leith. Home to a popular Sunday market and an unhurried, well-heeled charm.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the boutique shops and Sunday market, though parking is difficult and some spots feel pricey.
places.localTip: Come on a Sunday for the Stockbridge Market, then walk the Water of Leith path towards the Botanics.
places.story: Stockbridge grew up as a milling village on the Water of Leith before the city swallowed it whole, yet it has kept its own village soul. Its lanes are packed with delis, vintage shops and the famous 'Colonies' cottages built for Victorian workers. On Sundays the street market fills with cheese, flowers and street food, drawing locals from across the city.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Stockbridge Sunday Market (10am-5pm).

places.name: Water of Leith Walkway (Dean Village)
places.reviewSummary: A tranquil riverside path winding through Dean Village, a fairytale cluster of 19th-century millworkers' houses tucked in a gorge minutes from Princes Street. Astonishingly peaceful.
places.googleReviewSummary: Walkers are charmed by the storybook houses and quiet, though they remind visitors it is a residential area to respect.
places.localTip: Photograph Well Court from the bridge, then follow the river upstream to St Bernard's Well.
places.story: Dean Village was for centuries a busy milling community, grinding grain on the rushing Water of Leith below the city. When the mills fell silent, this pocket of red-roofed Tudor-style houses was left frozen in time, hidden in a leafy gorge. Today you can stroll the river path and barely believe the bustle of Princes Street is only a few minutes away.
places.highlight: Don't miss the picture-perfect Well Court courtyard.

places.name: The Sheep Heid Inn
places.reviewSummary: Reputedly Scotland's oldest pub, dating to 1360, in the picturesque village of Duddingston behind Arthur's Seat. Cosy fires, Sunday roasts and a Victorian skittle alley.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the historic charm and roasts, though it is a little out of the centre and busy at weekends.
places.localTip: Book the historic skittle alley for a game, and combine your visit with a walk over Arthur's Seat.
places.story: The Sheep Heid claims to have been serving since 1360, which would make it one of the oldest pubs in Scotland, and even Mary, Queen of Scots is said to have stopped by. Hidden in the conservation village of Duddingston behind Arthur's Seat, it still has a Victorian skittle alley out back. After a hike over the hill, its fires and ales feel richly earned.
places.highlight: Don't miss a game in the antique skittle alley.

places.name: Edinburgh Farmers' Market
places.reviewSummary: A long-running Saturday market beneath the castle rock, selling Scottish meats, cheeses, breads and street food directly from producers. A delicious local ritual.
places.googleReviewSummary: Shoppers praise the quality of local produce and the castle backdrop, though it is on the smaller side and weather-dependent.
places.localTip: Go hungry on a Saturday morning and graze your way along the stalls for breakfast.
places.story: Every Saturday, Castle Terrace fills with farmers and food makers from across Scotland, the castle rock looming dramatically overhead. You can buy venison, handmade cheese, just-baked bread and wild boar sausages straight from the people who made them. It is the kind of unhurried, producer-led market that locals build their weekend around.

places.name: Portobello Beach
places.reviewSummary: Edinburgh's sandy seaside suburb, with a long Victorian promenade, ice-cream shops, swimmers and a Turkish-baths spa. The city's beloved beach escape.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the relaxed seaside vibe and walks, though the North Sea is bracingly cold for swimming.
places.localTip: Walk the prom to Miller's ice cream, or be brave and join the hardy local sea swimmers.
places.story: Portobello, nicknamed 'Porty', was a fashionable Victorian seaside resort and still wears that gentle holiday air. Its sandy beach and mile-long promenade draw dog-walkers, paddlers and a growing tribe of cold-water swimmers year-round. The restored Portobello Turkish Baths offer a warm reward after a bracing dip in the Firth of Forth.
places.highlight: Don't miss an ice cream on the prom.

places.name: The Scotch Whisky Experience
places.reviewSummary: An immersive whisky tour beside the castle, complete with a barrel-ride through production and the world's largest collection of Scotch. A fun, accessible whisky primer.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests enjoy the tasting and the giant whisky collection, though purists find the barrel ride a touch gimmicky.
places.localTip: Upgrade to the Gold tour for a regional tasting flight that teaches you the differences between whisky regions.
places.story: Right by the castle, this attraction whisks you through whisky-making on a quirky barrel-car ride before a guided tasting. Its crowning glory is the Diageo Claive Vidiz Collection, the world's largest single collection of Scotch whisky, nearly 3,400 bottles glowing in a vast amber wall. It is the gentlest, most enjoyable way to learn why Scotland's water of life matters so much.
places.highlight: Don't miss the wall of 3,400 whisky bottles.

places.name: The Real Mary King's Close
places.address: 2 Warriston's Close, High Street, Edinburgh EH1 1PG
places.reviewSummary: A guided tour through real 17th-century streets and homes sealed beneath the City Chambers. Costumed guides bring the plague-era Old Town vividly, eerily to life.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find it fascinating and atmospheric, though it involves stairs and tight spaces unsuitable for the claustrophobic.
places.localTip: Book online in advance and choose an earlier slot — tours sell out and groups are kept small.
places.story: When Edinburgh's grand City Chambers were built in 1753, the engineers simply sealed the warren of streets beneath them, preserving a whole hidden Old Town. Today guides lead you down into those buried closes, past rooms where plague victims once lived and died. The tale of little Annie, whose 'haunted' room is piled with toys left by visitors, never fails to give people chills.
places.highlight: Don't miss Annie's haunted room.

places.name: Mercat Tours (Underground Vaults)
places.address: Mercat Cross, 28 High Street, Edinburgh EH1 1QS
places.reviewSummary: Edinburgh's longest-running tour company, leading historian-led walks and atmospheric trips into the hidden South Bridge Vaults. Smart storytelling over cheap scares.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the knowledgeable guides and genuine history, though the vaults are dark and damp by design.
places.localTip: Pick a history-led vault tour over a pure ghost tour if you want substance with your spookiness.
places.story: Mercat Tours starts at the Mercat Cross, the very spot where royal proclamations were read and criminals once pilloried. From there guides take you into the South Bridge Vaults, dank stone chambers that once hid the city's poorest and most desperate. Their storytelling leans on real research, making the hair-raising tales all the more convincing.

places.name: Camera Obscura & World of Illusions
places.reviewSummary: A wonderfully quirky five-floor attraction of optical illusions, mirror mazes and a Victorian camera obscura projecting a live image of the city. Great fun for all ages.
places.googleReviewSummary: Families love the hands-on illusions and rooftop views, though it can get crowded and busy on rainy days.
places.localTip: Time your visit so a guide demonstrates the live camera obscura — it is the magical original highlight.
places.story: At the top of the Royal Mile sits Edinburgh's oldest purpose-built attraction, opened in 1853. Its rooftop camera obscura uses lenses and mirrors to throw a live, moving image of the whole city onto a viewing table, a piece of Victorian wizardry that still delights. Below it sprawl floors of mind-bending illusions, from a vortex tunnel to a hall of mirrors.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rooftop terrace views over the Royal Mile.

places.name: Edinburgh Literary Pub Tour
places.address: The Beehive Inn, 18-20 Grassmarket, Edinburgh EH1 2JU
places.reviewSummary: A theatrical walking tour through Old Town pubs, led by two sparring actors performing the lives of Scotland's great writers. Witty, boozy and genuinely clever.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the actors' humour and literary insight, though the route involves a fair bit of walking between stops.
places.localTip: Buy a drink at each pub stop to settle in — the performances pause long enough to enjoy them.
places.story: Setting off from the Beehive Inn in the Grassmarket, two actors spar and perform their way through the haunts of Burns, Scott, Stevenson and Hugh MacDiarmid. Part comedy, part lecture, it captures Edinburgh's status as the world's first UNESCO City of Literature. By the end you have laughed, learned and visited several of the Old Town's most storied pubs.

places.name: Edinburgh Gin Distillery
places.address: 1a Rutland Place, West End, Edinburgh EH1 2AD
places.reviewSummary: A West End distillery experience where you tour the copper stills and taste a flight of gins beneath the bustle of Princes Street. Hands-on and good fun.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the engaging guides and generous tastings, though the cellar space is intimate and books up fast.
places.localTip: Book the gin-making 'make your own' session if you want a hands-on souvenir bottle to take home.
places.story: Edinburgh has a long, slightly riotous history with gin, and this distillery revives that spirit beneath the West End. In its copper stills, named Flora and Caledonia, botanicals from Scottish coast and country are distilled into their signature gins. The tasting cellar is snug and convivial, the kind of place an afternoon happily evaporates.

places.name: Scottish National Gallery
places.reviewSummary: Scotland's premier art collection on the Mound, with works by Botticelli, Titian, Vermeer, Monet and a peerless gathering of Scottish masters. Free and superbly displayed.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the world-class collection and free entry, though some found parts closed during recent gallery redevelopment.
places.localTip: Seek out the Scottish galleries downstairs for Raeburn's iconic 'Skating Minister' — a national treasure.
places.story: Housed in a grand neoclassical temple between the Old and New Towns, the gallery holds masterpieces from the Renaissance to Impressionism. Its most famous resident is Raeburn's 'Reverend Robert Walker Skating on Duddingston Loch', a serene minister gliding across the ice that has become an icon of Scottish art. Best of all, entry to the permanent collection is completely free.
places.highlight: Don't miss Raeburn's 'Skating Minister'.

places.name: Scottish National Gallery of Modern Art
places.reviewSummary: Two grand buildings in parkland west of the centre, showing Picasso, Dali, Hockney and Scottish modernists. The lawns feature a striking Charles Jencks landform.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the art and the sculptural grounds, though it is a walk or bus ride from the centre.
places.localTip: Walk here along the Water of Leith from Stockbridge, then relax on the grass swirls of the landform.
places.story: Set in two neoclassical mansions amid sweeping lawns, this is Scotland's home for modern and contemporary art. Out front, landscape designer Charles Jencks sculpted the earth itself into a sinuous, stepped landform around a reflecting pool. Inside you will find Dali, Picasso and a celebrated Surrealist collection, all reached by a lovely riverside walk.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Charles Jencks landform on the front lawn.

places.name: Scottish National Portrait Gallery
places.reviewSummary: A stunning red-sandstone Gothic gallery telling Scotland's story through the faces of its people, from Mary, Queen of Scots to modern icons. The Great Hall frieze alone is worth the trip.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the building and varied portraits, though some wish for more explanation beside individual works.
places.localTip: Look up in the entrance hall to follow the painted frieze of famous Scots, then visit the photography galleries upstairs.
places.story: This red Gothic palace on Queen Street was the world's first purpose-built portrait gallery, opened in 1889 and paid for by a newspaper magnate. Its breathtaking entrance hall is wrapped in a golden frieze of 155 great Scots and a starry ceiling. Inside, the faces of monarchs, poets, scientists and rebels trace the whole sweep of Scottish history.
places.highlight: Don't miss the gilded Great Hall and its processional frieze.

places.name: Fruitmarket Gallery
places.reviewSummary: A leading contemporary art space beside Waverley Station, in a former fruit market, with bold exhibitions, a great bookshop and a buzzy café. Free and consistently interesting.
places.googleReviewSummary: Art-goers appreciate the ambitious shows and café, though the gallery is compact so visits are fairly short.
places.localTip: Browse the excellent art bookshop and grab coffee even if the current show is not your thing.
places.story: Housed in a 1930s fruit-and-veg market right by Waverley Station, the Fruitmarket has grown into one of Scotland's most respected contemporary galleries. Its airy, light-filled spaces have shown everyone from Turner Prize winners to emerging local talent. With free entry, a fine bookshop and a lively café, it is a creative pit-stop in the heart of town.

places.name: Royal Lyceum Theatre
places.reviewSummary: Edinburgh's grand Victorian producing theatre, staging classic and new drama in a gorgeous gilded auditorium. The city's home of serious stagecraft.
places.googleReviewSummary: Theatregoers love the historic auditorium and quality productions, though leg-room in the older seats is tight.
places.localTip: Check for their Christmas show, a beloved Edinburgh family tradition that sells out early.
places.story: Opened in 1883 and named after Henry Irving's London theatre, the Lyceum is one of Scotland's most important producing houses. Its plush, horseshoe-shaped Victorian auditorium wraps the audience close to the stage. Generations of Edinburgh families have had their first taste of live theatre here, often at its famous festive productions.

places.name: Cameo Picturehouse
places.reviewSummary: A beloved independent cinema from 1914, showing arthouse, indie and cult films with a licensed bar. Atmospheric Screen One is a listed Edwardian gem.
places.googleReviewSummary: Film fans cherish the historic screens and curated programme, though seats in the oldest auditorium are vintage-cosy.
places.localTip: Book Screen One for the full experience, and arrive early for a drink in the front bar.
places.story: The Cameo has been showing films since 1914, making it one of the oldest continually running cinemas in Scotland. Quentin Tarantino has named it among his favourite cinemas in the world, and its ornate main screen is protected as a listed interior. It remains the spiritual home of Edinburgh's film lovers, all red velvet and flickering magic.
places.highlight: Don't miss a film in the historic Screen One.

places.name: Stills Centre for Photography
places.reviewSummary: A long-established photography gallery and darkroom on winding Cockburn Street, with free exhibitions and analogue facilities. A quiet creative gem.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors appreciate the thoughtful shows and free entry, though the gallery is small and easy to miss.
places.localTip: Ask about their darkroom courses if you shoot film — it is one of the few public darkrooms left in the city.
places.story: Founded in 1977, Stills has championed photography in Edinburgh for nearly half a century. Tucked on serpentine Cockburn Street, it pairs free exhibitions with darkrooms and digital labs that the public can actually use. It is the sort of unshowy, mission-driven space that keeps a city's creative heart beating.

places.name: Sandy Bell's
places.reviewSummary: A legendary folk pub near the university where impromptu traditional music sessions run nightly. Cramped, joyful and utterly authentic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are enchanted by the live fiddles and friendly crowd, though it is tiny and standing-room-only when sessions get going.
places.localTip: Arrive early evening to grab a spot near the musicians, and never clap mid-tune-set — wait for the end.
places.story: Sandy Bell's has been the beating heart of Edinburgh's folk scene since the mid-20th century, a place where the city's traditional music never really stopped. Fiddlers, pipers and singers gather round the corner of the bar most nights for sessions that are free and gloriously unplanned. Many of Scotland's finest folk musicians cut their teeth in this very room.
places.highlight: Don't miss the nightly trad session.

places.name: Usher Hall
places.reviewSummary: Edinburgh's grand domed concert hall from 1914, prized for superb acoustics and a sweeping programme from orchestras to touring rock acts. The city's premier music venue.
places.googleReviewSummary: Concertgoers consistently praise the acoustics and beautiful interior, though some upper-tier seats have restricted views.
places.localTip: For classical concerts, the stalls and grand circle give the best blend of the famous acoustics.
places.story: The Usher Hall was a gift to the city from whisky distiller Andrew Usher and opened in 1914, its great Beaux-Arts dome a New Town landmark. Renowned for some of the finest acoustics in the UK, it hosts everything from the Royal Scottish National Orchestra to global pop stars. During the Edinburgh International Festival it becomes the city's musical centrepiece.

places.name: La Belle Angele
places.reviewSummary: A storied Old Town venue hidden off the Cowgate, hosting indie gigs and club nights. Reborn after a fire, it remains a cornerstone of the city's grassroots scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Gig-goers love the intimate stage and sound, though it can get hot and very busy when packed.
places.localTip: Follow its gig listings closely — touring bands often play here just before they get big.
places.story: La Belle Angele has been part of Edinburgh's underground music story for decades, surviving the devastating 2002 Cowgate fire to rise again. Tucked down a hidden close, it has hosted everyone from local upstarts to internationally touring acts. It is exactly the kind of sweaty, low-ceilinged room where great gigs are remembered for life.

places.name: The Jazz Bar
places.reviewSummary: A basement bar dedicated to live jazz, blues, funk and soul, with multiple sets daily until the early hours. Run by musicians, for music lovers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the genuine musicianship and late hours, though the basement is snug and gets warm when full.
places.localTip: Late-night weekend sets bring the funk and soul bands — stay past midnight for the best energy.
places.story: Founded by a local jazz drummer, The Jazz Bar is the city's only dedicated jazz venue, running live music from afternoon into the small hours. Its low brick-vaulted basement on Chambers Street is intimate enough that you feel part of the band. From cool trios to high-energy funk, it keeps Edinburgh's jazz tradition swinging nightly.
places.highlight: Don't miss a late-night weekend funk set.

places.name: The Queen's Hall
places.reviewSummary: A converted Georgian church on the Southside, now an intimate concert venue for folk, jazz, classical and singer-songwriters. Wonderful sightlines and acoustics.
places.googleReviewSummary: Audiences praise the atmosphere and intimacy, though the old church pews can be hard on the back for long shows.
places.localTip: Book balcony seats for the best view down onto the stage in this former kirk.
places.story: Built in 1823 as Hope Park Chapel, this elegant Georgian church found new life in 1979 as a beloved concert hall. Its galleried interior brings audiences remarkably close to the performers, making it a favourite for folk legends and chamber ensembles alike. The home venue of the Scottish Chamber Orchestra, it punches far above its modest size.

places.name: Sneaky Pete's
places.reviewSummary: A tiny, world-renowned one-room club on the Cowgate, packing in cult DJs and live bands nightly. Sweaty, loud and beloved by serious clubbers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Clubbers rave about the booking and atmosphere, though everyone agrees it is small and gets shoulder-to-shoulder full.
places.localTip: Check the listings before you go — the music swings wildly from techno to indie depending on the night.
places.story: Sneaky Pete's may be one of the smallest clubs in Edinburgh, but its reputation is enormous, regularly named among the UK's best small venues. In a single sweaty room on the notorious Cowgate, it programmes everything from underground techno to live indie, seven nights a week. To dance here is to feel the true pulse of Edinburgh's nightlife.

places.name: The Bow Bar
places.address: 80 West Bow, Victoria Street, Edinburgh EH1 2HH
places.reviewSummary: A traditional one-room pub on Victoria Street with a wall of 300-plus whiskies and superb cask ales. No music, no TV, just great drinks and conversation.
places.googleReviewSummary: Drinkers love the whisky range and old-school charm, though it is small and fills fast in the evenings.
places.localTip: Ask the bar staff to recommend a dram by region or flavour — they genuinely know the whole back wall.
places.story: The Bow Bar is a perfectly preserved traditional Edinburgh pub on the curve of Victoria Street, all dark wood, brass and antique mirrors. Behind the bar stands a glittering wall of over 300 whiskies alongside rotating cask ales from across Scotland. With no music or screens, it is built for the lost arts of a quiet dram and good conversation.
places.highlight: Don't miss the back wall of 300+ whiskies.

places.name: Bramble Bar
places.reviewSummary: A hidden basement cocktail bar beneath Queen Street, repeatedly ranked among the world's best bars. Inventive drinks in a candlelit warren of stone nooks.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the creative cocktails and intimate vibe, though the small space means it gets packed late and may have a queue.
places.localTip: Go early in the week or before 9pm to grab one of the cosy stone alcoves without queuing.
places.story: Down an unmarked basement stair beneath Queen Street, Bramble has quietly become one of the most celebrated cocktail bars in the world. Its labyrinth of low stone arches feels like a secret you were lucky to be let in on. The bartending is seriously inventive, yet the mood stays unpretentious and warm.

places.name: The Liquid Room
places.reviewSummary: A long-standing club and live-music venue just off the Grassmarket, hosting big club nights and touring bands. A reliable cornerstone of Old Town nightlife.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the sound system and central location, though it can get very busy and warm on big club nights.
places.localTip: Its student nights are huge value, but the live-band gigs are where the venue really shines.
places.story: The Liquid Room sits beneath Victoria Street and has been a fixture of Edinburgh nightlife for decades, blending club nights with live gigs. Its booming sound system has hosted everyone from breaking indie acts to established names. Right in the Old Town's nightlife heartland, it is where many a long Edinburgh night gathers pace.

places.name: The Hanging Bat
places.reviewSummary: A buzzy craft-beer bar on Lothian Road with a huge rotating tap list, its own brewing kit and smoked BBQ food. A hub for Edinburgh's beer geeks.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beer fans love the range and knowledgeable staff, though it can get loud and crowded at weekends.
places.localTip: Ask for a tasting paddle to sample several of the constantly changing keg and cask beers.
places.story: The Hanging Bat helped lead Edinburgh's craft-beer revolution, lining up a constantly changing wall of taps from the most exciting breweries around. It even brews on a tiny in-house kit and smokes its own BBQ to soak it all up. Equal parts beer hall and laboratory, it is a pilgrimage spot for anyone who takes their pint seriously.

places.name: Cold Town House
places.reviewSummary: A multi-level brewpub in the Grassmarket with its own brewery, wood-fired pizza and a rooftop terrace facing Edinburgh Castle. Great for a drink with a view.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the castle-view rooftop and house beers, though the rooftop is weather-dependent and books up fast.
places.localTip: Head straight for the rooftop on a clear evening — the castle view at golden hour is unbeatable.
places.story: Cold Town House takes its name from the old name for the castle's south side and brews its beer right in the building. Its big draw is the open rooftop terrace, which stares straight up at Edinburgh Castle looming over the Grassmarket. With wood-fired pizza and house lagers, it is a modern crowd-pleaser in a deeply historic square.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rooftop castle view at sunset.

places.name: The Cafe Royal
places.reviewSummary: A breathtaking Victorian pub near Princes Street, all marble, mahogany and tiled portrait murals. As much a heritage interior as a place to drink.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are wowed by the ornate interior and atmosphere, though it can be busy and the food is pricier than a standard pub.
places.localTip: Step into the oval Circle Bar just to admire the Doulton tile murals, even if only for one drink.
places.story: The Café Royal has dazzled drinkers since 1863 with its lavish Victorian interior of stained glass, marble and Doulton ceramic murals depicting great inventors. The room is so beautiful it is protected as a listed interior and has appeared in films like Chariots of Fire. Stop in for a pint of stout and oysters and you are drinking inside a working museum.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Doulton tile portrait murals.

places.name: CC Blooms
places.reviewSummary: Edinburgh's best-known LGBTQ+ bar and club at the top of Leith Walk, with two floors of pop, drag and dancing. The lively heart of the city's gay scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the inclusive, high-energy dancefloor and drag nights, though it gets very busy and sweaty at the weekend.
places.localTip: It is free to get in, so start your night here and let the basement dancefloor fill up after 11pm.
places.story: Named with a wink after a Bette Midler character, CC Blooms has been the cornerstone of Edinburgh's LGBTQ+ nightlife for over 30 years. Upstairs is a friendly bar, downstairs a pumping dancefloor of pop and drag near the top of Leith Walk. Welcoming and gloriously unpretentious, it is where the city comes to let loose.

places.name: Johnnie Walker Princes Street (Blending Workshop)
places.reviewSummary: A spectacular eight-floor whisky visitor centre on Princes Street offering guided tastings and hands-on blending sessions, topped by a rooftop bar with castle views.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the slick experience and rooftop views, though some feel it is more polished show than traditional distillery.
places.localTip: Book a personalised flavour experience first, then head up to the rooftop bar for the views.
places.story: Opened in 2021 inside a former department store, this is Johnnie Walker's flagship celebration of the world's best-selling Scotch. Across eight floors you can blend your own whisky, taste flights tailored to your palate, and learn the story of a brand born in an Ayrshire grocer's shop. Crown it all with a cocktail on the rooftop bar, gazing across to the castle.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rooftop bar after your tasting.

places.name: Edinburgh New Town Cookery School
places.reviewSummary: A respected cookery school in a Georgian townhouse offering hands-on day classes from Scottish cooking to knife skills. Polished, friendly and genuinely instructive.
places.googleReviewSummary: Students praise the expert tuition and welcoming kitchens, though popular classes book up well in advance.
places.localTip: Look for their shorter evening or single-day classes if you only have a little time in the city.
places.story: Set in an elegant New Town townhouse on Queen Street, this school trains everyone from career chefs to curious holidaymakers. Its hands-on classes might have you breaking down a Scottish salmon or mastering the perfect shortbread. You leave with new skills, a full belly and recipes to take home.

places.name: The Pottery Studio Edinburgh
places.reviewSummary: A welcoming Leith ceramics studio running wheel-throwing and hand-building taster classes for all levels. A calm, creative few hours away from the tourist trail.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants love the relaxed teaching and getting muddy on the wheel, though kiln-firing means finished pieces take weeks to collect or post.
places.localTip: Book a taster wheel session and arrange postage in advance, as fired pieces are ready weeks later.
places.story: Tucked among the creative warehouses of Leith, this studio invites total beginners to sit at the wheel and get gloriously messy with clay. Patient teachers guide you through throwing a bowl or mug in a calm, light-filled space. It is a lovely antidote to a packed sightseeing itinerary, and you go home having actually made something.

places.name: Tartan Weaving Mill & Exhibition
places.reviewSummary: A working weaving mill at the top of the Royal Mile where you can watch tartan being made, trace your clan and even try weaving. A hands-on slice of Scottish heritage.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy seeing the looms and the clan history, though the lower floors lean heavily into the gift shop.
places.localTip: Head down to the basement mill floor to see and hear the giant looms actually weaving.
places.story: Right beside the castle, this mill lets you watch tartan being woven on clattering looms just as it has been for centuries. You can search the clan archives for your family name, learn how a sett of colours becomes a recognised tartan, and even try the weaving yourself. Beneath the inevitable shop lies a genuinely fascinating piece of living Scottish craft.

places.name: Calton Hill
places.reviewSummary: A small hill crowned with neoclassical monuments offering the classic postcard panorama of the city, castle and Firth of Forth. Edinburgh's premier sunset spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call the views and monuments stunning, though it gets crowded at sunset and is exposed to the wind.
places.localTip: Come for sunset and shoot back towards the castle and Old Town skyline with the National Monument in frame.
places.story: Calton Hill is dotted with grand Greek-revival monuments that earned Edinburgh its nickname, the 'Athens of the North'. The unfinished National Monument, modelled on the Parthenon, was abandoned for lack of funds and dubbed 'Edinburgh's Disgrace', yet it now makes a spectacular silhouette. From here the whole city spreads beneath you, making it the most photographed viewpoint in town.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunset over the Old Town skyline.

places.name: Victoria Street (West Bow)
places.reviewSummary: A steeply curving, rainbow-coloured row of shopfronts said to have inspired Diagon Alley. The most photogenic street in Edinburgh.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors adore the colourful curve and shops, though it gets very crowded with photographers, especially midday.
places.localTip: Shoot from the Upper Bow terrace above for the famous sweeping curved view down the street.
places.story: Victoria Street was carved out in the 1830s as a graceful curved improvement to the old, steep West Bow. Its tiers of brightly painted shops are widely rumoured to have inspired J.K. Rowling's Diagon Alley, and Harry Potter fans flock here in droves. For the best photo, climb to the terrace above and capture the whole rainbow sweep at once.
places.highlight: Don't miss the view from the Upper Bow terrace.

places.name: Dean Village (Well Court)
places.reviewSummary: A fairytale cluster of red-roofed former mill buildings in a wooded gorge on the Water of Leith. One of the most enchanting photo spots in the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the storybook setting, though many stress it is a quiet residential area to be respected.
places.localTip: Shoot Well Court from the bridge on Hawthornbank Lane for the classic framed composition.
places.story: Hidden in a steep green gorge just minutes from Princes Street, Dean Village feels like stepping into a fairy tale. Its photogenic centrepiece, Well Court, was built in the 1880s as model housing for local workers, with a clock tower and courtyard straight from a storybook. The rushing Water of Leith and overhanging trees complete one of Edinburgh's most magical scenes.
places.highlight: Don't miss Well Court reflected in the river.

places.name: Vennel Steps Viewpoint
places.reviewSummary: A narrow stone stairway near the Grassmarket framing a perfect view of Edinburgh Castle above the old Flodden Wall. A favourite hidden photo spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the framed castle shot, though the popular spot can have a short queue of photographers at golden hour.
places.localTip: Arrive at golden hour for warm light on the castle, and look for the surviving Flodden Wall beside the steps.
places.story: The Vennel is a slim flight of steps linking the Grassmarket to the Old Town, and partway up it offers a flawless framed view of Edinburgh Castle. Beside the steps stands a rare surviving stretch of the Flodden Wall, built to defend the city after a crushing defeat in 1513. Photographers have made this quiet stair one of the city's most-loved viewpoints.
places.highlight: Don't miss the castle framed between the old walls.

places.name: North Bridge Viewpoint
places.reviewSummary: A grand Victorian bridge linking the Old and New Towns, with sweeping views over Waverley Station to the Balmoral clock tower and Calton Hill. A superb skyline vantage.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the panoramic views in both directions, though traffic noise and wind on the bridge can be intense.
places.localTip: Shoot east towards the Balmoral Hotel's clock tower, then west for the dramatic Old Town rooftops.
places.story: North Bridge soars over the valley where the old Nor Loch once stood, drained to build the New Town. From its pavements you get one of the city's great free panoramas: trains sliding into Waverley, the gothic spire of the Scott Monument, and Calton Hill beyond. The famous Balmoral clock is kept three minutes fast so travellers never miss their train.
places.highlight: Don't miss the view towards the Balmoral clock tower.

places.name: Greyfriars Kirkyard
places.reviewSummary: An atmospheric historic graveyard famed for Greyfriars Bobby, Harry Potter name inspirations and moody views towards the castle. Eerie, beautiful and rich in stories.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the atmosphere and Potter connections, though some find the ghost-tour crowds detract from the peace.
places.localTip: Find the grave of 'Thomas Riddell' that reputedly inspired Voldemort's name, then shoot the castle through the trees.
places.story: Greyfriars Kirkyard is one of the most storied graveyards in the world, where a loyal little dog named Bobby supposedly guarded his master's grave for 14 years. J.K. Rowling, writing nearby, drew names from its weathered headstones, including a Thomas Riddell. By day it is a poignant green oasis; after dark, its tales of body-snatchers and hauntings give it a deliciously eerie chill.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Greyfriars Bobby statue at the kirkyard gate.
